narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 68% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the typical student's 5.71; your even lower Year 3 decile of 2 compared to the average 5.53; your LSAT score of 29 versus the usual 36.7; your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 against an average of 3.21; and your lower-middle family income bracket, which is below the dataset's average of 3.47. You're also a Black male planning full-time enrollment, which aligns with common patterns—males make up 56.4% of applicants, Black students 4.6%, and full-time 92.7%—but didn't sway the outcome much. The model's decision was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, likely because such a low standing signals challenges in sustaining academic performance. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which similarly nudged the model toward expecting bar struggles due to being well below what succeeds for most students. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, reinforcing concerns about early academic footing. Family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender had progressively smaller roles, all contributing toward the failure prediction but to lesser degrees. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your grades and test scores stand out as notably lower, putting you in the lower tail of the distribution where failure risks rise. While demographics like race and gender played minor parts, your academic metrics dominated the reasoning. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as it's a key factor and often improvable with targeted prep. Strengthening study habits or seeking academic support could help mirror the trajectories of higher-decile students who pass. Building a stronger post-undergrad record, if possible, might also offset past GPAs in similar models. With dedication, you can shift these predictors in your favor for another shot at admission. Keep pushing—you've got potential to rewrite this story.
